LABEL: constitutional history
RATIONALE: Directly traces the evolution of Roman Republican institutions, magistracies, and constitutional arrangements as expressions of political power.

LABEL: constitutional law
RATIONALE: Covers the formal structures of Republican governance — consular authority, senatorial supremacy, and the distribution of sovereign power.

LABEL: civil and political rights
RATIONALE: Citizenship as a political status (civitas) was the foundational category determining participation in Roman Republican governance.

LABEL: geopolitics
RATIONALE: The Republic's expansion, provincial governance, and interstate relations with Carthage, Greece, and the East are core political phenomena.

LABEL: economic history
RATIONALE: Included only insofar as fiscal policy, grain supply (annona), and land redistribution debates (Gracchan reforms) were instruments of political contestation.

LABEL: auxiliary science of history
RATIONALE: Provides methodological tools (epigraphy, numismatics as political propaganda) for reconstructing Republican political history.

LABEL: administrative law
RATIONALE: Provincial administration and magisterial imperium represent political control mechanisms, making administrative law politically relevant here.

LABEL: Political theory — General and comparative
RATIONALE: Covers theories of sovereignty, republicanism, and political authority directly applicable to the Roman Republic's governmental model.

LABEL: Geopolitics
RATIONALE: Encompasses Roman territorial expansion, hegemony over Italy, and imperial projection as political phenomena.

LABEL: Civil and political rights
RATIONALE: Political citizenship (civitas Romana) and its gradual extension were central political events of the Republican period.

LABEL: Political institutions — Europe
RATIONALE: Houses scholarship on Roman Republican constitutional arrangements and their European institutional legacy.

LABEL: History of Italy — Rome (Ancient)
RATIONALE: The primary LCC location for historical and political narrative of the Roman Republic, including senatorial politics and civil wars.

LABEL: Law — General
RATIONALE: Included selectively where law functions as a political weapon — e.g., quaestiones perpetuae and proscription legislation.

LABEL: Constitutional history and law — Ancient
RATIONALE: Directly relevant to the formal constitutional arrangements of the Roman Republic including the cursus honorum and tribunicia potestas.

LABEL: Rome — History — Republic, 510–30 B.C.
RATIONALE: The canonical LCSH heading precisely delimiting the political history of the Roman Republic as a discrete governmental era.

LABEL: Constitutional history
RATIONALE: Covers the historical development of Republican constitutional structures including consuls, Senate, tribunes, and popular assemblies.

LABEL: Constitutional law
RATIONALE: Formal legal-political framework of Republican magistracies, vetoes, and the lex as an instrument of political sovereignty.

LABEL: Civil and political rights
RATIONALE: Citizenship extension (Social War, Latin rights) was a defining political conflict of the late Republic.

LABEL: Geopolitics
RATIONALE: Roman Republican foreign policy, provincial creation, and interstate dominance are geopolitical expressions of political power.

LABEL: Diplomatic history
RATIONALE: Treaties, alliances (foedera), and Roman diplomatic supremacy were political instruments of the Republic's power system.

LABEL: History, Military
RATIONALE: Included only where military command (imperium) and triumphs functioned as political power resources, not for operational military history per se.

LABEL: History
RATIONALE: General historical heading anchoring the Republican period's political narrative within the broader Roman historical record.

LABEL: Colonies — History
RATIONALE: Roman colonization was a deliberate political tool for controlling territory and extending citizen communities.

LABEL: Historical geography
RATIONALE: The political map of Republican Rome — provincial boundaries, allied territories, and voting districts (tribus) — is essential political geography.

LABEL: Roman Republic
RATIONALE: The primary subject entity — an aristocratic republic representing a distinct form of government and historical political period.

LABEL: Roman Senate
RATIONALE: The dominant political institution of the Republic, controlling foreign policy, finance, and provincial governance.

LABEL: Citizens' assemblies of the Roman Republic
RATIONALE: The comitia and concilium plebis were the sovereign legislative and electoral bodies expressing popular political will.

LABEL: Roman emperor
RATIONALE: Relevant as the institutional endpoint whose emergence marks the political collapse of the Republican system.

LABEL: Ancient Rome
RATIONALE: The encompassing political civilisation within which the Republican form of government operated and evolved.

LABEL: Rome
RATIONALE: The seat of Republican political institutions — Senate, Forum Romanum, and all central magistracies were located here.

LABEL: Caesar's Civil War
RATIONALE: The defining political crisis that terminated the Republican constitution and demonstrated the failure of its power-sharing mechanisms.

LABEL: Ancient Carthage
RATIONALE: Rome's primary political and military rival, whose defeat structured Roman Republican imperialism and provincial expansion.

LABEL: Deposition of Romulus Augustulus
RATIONALE: Marks the formal end of the Western Roman political tradition descended from the Republic, relevant as a terminal political boundary.

LABEL: Carthage
RATIONALE: The city whose political destruction (146 BCE) marked Rome's unchallenged political hegemony in the Mediterranean.

LABEL: Athens
RATIONALE: Politically significant as a model for Republican constitutional thought and as a subject of Roman political subordination after 146 BCE.

LABEL: Byzantium
RATIONALE: Relevant as a Greek polis under Republican political control and later as the site of imperial political continuity.
